l-hello.title: The magic question ending: -aano?
l-hello.intro: Malayalam turns a statement into a yes/no question just by adding -aano? to the end. No word order changes — much simpler than English!

points.heading: Statement → Question
points.body: ‘sukhamaanu’ = (you) are well. Add the question ending and it becomes ‘sukhamaano?’ = are you well?

points.heading: Hindi shortcut
points.body: Many words share roots with Hindi: ‘sukham’ = well-being (Hindi ‘sukh’), ‘nandi’ = thanks. Lean on what you already know.

l-yesno.title: Saying 'I want': enikku … venam
l-yesno.intro: To say you want something, Malayalam puts YOU in the dative ('to me') and ends with 'venam' (is wanted) — exactly like Hindi 'mujhe … chahiye'.

points.heading: The pattern
points.body: enikku (to me) + [thing] + venam (want). Drop 'venam' for 'venda' to say you DON'T want it.

points.heading: Two ways to say 'no'
points.body: ‘alla’ = it is NOT (that). ‘illa’ = there isn't / doesn't exist. Use ‘illa’ for absence: ‘vellam illa’ = there's no water.

l-clarify.title: Adding -illa to mean 'not'
l-clarify.intro: Stick -illa onto a verb to negate it. This single trick lets you flip any phrase you know into its negative.

points.heading: Flip it negative
points.body: ‘manassilaayi’ = (I) understood → ‘manassilaayilla’ = (I) didn't understand. ‘ariyaam’ = (I) know → ‘ariyilla’ = (I) don't know.

l-intro.title: 'My' and 'is': ente … aanu
l-intro.intro: Malayalam puts the verb at the END (Subject-Object-Verb). 'aanu' means 'is/am/are' and closes the sentence.

points.heading: Possessives
points.body: ‘ente’ = my, ‘ningalude’ = your. The owner comes first: ‘ente per’ = my name.

points.heading: Verb goes last
points.body: English: 'My name IS Ravi.' Malayalam: 'My name Ravi IS' — the 'aanu' (is) lands at the end. This SOV order is the #1 thing to get used to.

l-about-you.title: He, she, we
l-about-you.intro: Malayalam pronouns are simple, but politeness matters. Use 'ningal' (polite you) with anyone you don't know well.

points.heading: The basics
points.body: njaan = I, ningal = you (polite), avan = he, aval = she, nammal = we (including you).

l-restaurant.title: Polite requests: -aamo? and joining with -um
l-restaurant.intro: End a request with -aamo? to sound polite ('could you…?'), and link nouns with -um … -um ('both … and').

points.heading: Could you…?
points.body: ‘tharuka’ = give → ‘tharaamo?’ = could you give? Soft and polite, perfect for ordering.

points.heading: 'And' = -um … -um
points.body: Attach -um to each item: ‘choru-m saambaar-um’ = rice AND sambar.

l-directions.title: Where is …? and giving directions
l-directions.intro: Ask the location of anything with '… evideyaanu?' — just put the place at the front.

points.heading: The all-purpose question
points.body: [place] + evideyaanu? = where is [place]? Reuse it everywhere.

l-numbers.title: Counting in Malayalam
l-numbers.intro: The first ten numbers unlock prices, time, and quantities. A few sound close to other Indian languages.

points.heading: 1 to 10
points.body: onnu, randu, moonnu, naalu, anchu, aaru, ezhu, ettu, onpathu, pathu. Say them out loud — rhythm helps them stick.

l-bignum.title: Bigger numbers & bargaining
l-bignum.intro: ‘pathu’ (10), ‘irupathu’ (20), ‘nooru’ (100). Combine with the price question to shop confidently.

points.heading: How much?
points.body: ‘ithu ethra?’ = how much is this? If it's steep: ‘valare kooduthalaanu’ (too much) then ‘kuravu cheyyaamo?’ (reduce it?).

l-feelings.title: Feelings: enikku … aanu / undu
l-feelings.intro: Feelings also use the dative 'enikku' (to me). Pair it with 'aanu' (is) or 'undu' (there is/have).

points.heading: The pattern
points.body: enikku + [feeling] + aanu/undu. ‘enikku santhosham aanu’ = I'm happy. ‘enikku pedi undu’ = I'm scared.

l-health.title: Describing symptoms
l-health.intro: Same 'enikku … undu' pattern handles aches and illness — very useful at a pharmacy.

points.heading: I have a …
points.body: enikku + [symptom] + undu. ‘enikku pani undu’ = I have a fever. ‘enikku thalaveedana undu’ = I have a headache.

l-time.title: Talking about time
l-time.intro: A handful of time words let you make plans: innu (today), naale (tomorrow), innale (yesterday), ippol (now).

points.heading: When?
points.body: Drop these in front of any sentence. ‘naale pokaam’ = let's go tomorrow.

l-plans.title: Let's…: namukku … pokaam
l-plans.intro: Suggest doing something together with 'namukku' (let's) + verb ending in -aam.

points.heading: Make a suggestion
points.body: ‘namukku pokaam’ = let's go. Add a place: ‘namukku beach-il pokaam’ = let's go to the beach.
